ontology.foodex2_description: Rice flour (glutinous/waxy rice flour, milled from Oryza sativa var. glutinosa)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for glutinous rice flour as a food substance; SNOMED CT coverage for this ingredient is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of glutinous rice flour used in home cooking or baking is approximately 10 g (1 tablespoon), though recipes for traditional desserts such as tang yuan or mochi commonly call for 50–100 g per portion.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous Rice Flour — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, and iron taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for glutinous rice flour (cr_id F05040007). Sugar and saturated fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for rice flour, glutinous (NDB 20068) to complete secondary nutrient fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glutinous rice flour GI is estimated at approximately 98 based on published GI values for glutinous/waxy rice (GI ~98) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glutinous rice flour is milled from the same waxy rice grain and retains the near-pure amylopectin starch structure responsible for the very high GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (98 × 8.275) / 100 ≈ 8.1 for a 10 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (14 mg/100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Glutinous rice flour is suitable for individuals with hypertension as a raw ingredient, with sodium at only 14 mg/100 g; however, total dish sodium must be monitored when salt, soy sauce, or other high-sodium condiments are added during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~98) and high refined carbohydrate load (82.75 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glutinous rice flour should be used only in very small quantities in meal plans for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as its near-pure amylopectin starch structure produces a very high glycemic response (GI ~98); combining it with high-fibre or protein-rich ingredients can partially attenuate postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat content (0 g/100 g), very low sodium (14 mg/100 g), and no cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Glutinous rice flour poses minimal direct cardiovascular risk as a raw ingredient due to its negligible fat and very low sodium content; however, its high glycemic load and refined carbohydrate density may contribute to postprandial triglyceride elevation and weight gain if consumed in large quantities, which are indirect cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-glycemic refined starch with no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, polyphenols, or meaningful fibre)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Glutinous rice flour is not recommended as a staple ingredient in rheumatoid arthritis meal planning because its high glycemic load (GI ~98, 82.75 g carbs/100 g, 0.8 g fibre/100 g) promotes systemic inflammatory load without providing any compensating anti-inflammatory factors; whole-grain or legume-based flours with higher fibre content are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Glutinous rice flour (also known as sweet rice flour, sticky rice flour, or waxy rice flour) is a fine white powder milled from glutinous rice (Oryza sativa var. glutinosa), a short-grain rice variety whose starch is composed almost entirely of amylopectin with negligible amylose. This near-pure amylopectin composition gives the flour its characteristic sticky, chewy, and elastic texture when cooked with water, distinguishing it from regular rice flour. It is a staple ingredient across East and Southeast Asian cuisines, used to make traditional desserts and snacks such as tang yuan (Chinese glutinous rice balls), mochi (Japanese rice cakes), nian gao (Chinese New Year cake), onde-onde (Malay/Indonesian pandan-filled balls), and various Singaporean kueh. Nutritionally, glutinous rice flour is a high-carbohydrate, low-fat, low-protein ingredient providing approximately 357 kcal per 100 g, with 82.75 g carbohydrates, 6.0 g protein, and 0 g fat per 100 g. Dietary fibre is very low at 0.8 g/100 g and sodium is minimal at 14 mg/100 g. Its glycemic index is very high (approximately 98), making it one of the highest-GI staple flours available; this is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. In Singapore and the broader Southeast Asian region, it is widely used in both home cooking and commercial food production for traditional festive and everyday snacks.
common_user_queries: What is glutinous rice flour used for?
Is glutinous rice flour the same as regular rice flour?
What is the glycemic index of glutinous rice flour?
Can diabetics eat glutinous rice flour?
What is the difference between glutinous rice flour and tapioca flour?
How many calories in glutinous rice flour?
What is 糯米粉 used for?
meal_context: Festive foods (Chinese New Year, Mid-Autumn Festival)
Malay and Indonesian onde-onde and kuih
Thickening agent in soups and sauces

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05040007, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20068, rice flour glutinous). GI value supported by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous/waxy rice. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a milled glutinous rice product.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source (F05040007); glutinous rice flour is a refined starch product with negligible fat content — confirmed zero by formulation and source reporting. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero consistent with zero total fat. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived grain flour. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived product. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived grain flour with no fortification reported. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy NDB 20068 reports negligible values but HPB is the primary source and does not disaggregate fat fractions. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for glutinous rice flour and are not consistently available in USDA SR Legacy for this specific product. Sugar value of 0.3 g/100 g is estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 20068 (rice flour, white) as HPB does not report sugar separately; this is a minor component. Potassium (30 mg), calcium (6 mg), phosphorus (56 mg), magnesium (6 mg), zinc (0.7 mg), thiamine (0.02 mg), riboflavin (0.02 mg), niacin (0.4 mg), and water (10.5 g) values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 20068 as HPB does not report these fields. GI of 98 is an analogous estimate based on published GI for glutinous/waxy rice grain per Atkinson et al. 2008. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is the best available match; FoodEx2 code A000E is used as the closest defensible match for rice flour in the FoodEx2 hierarchy — reviewers should verify against the latest FoodEx2 release. SNOMED CT does not have a specific code for glutinous rice flour as a food substance.
